steps.action: Like much other music of Sub-Saharan Africa, it with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Like much other music of Sub-Saharan Africa, it with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: Makossa, which in some accounts is said to mean
steps.text: Makossa, which in some accounts is said to mean "the contortions" and others to mean "(I) dance" in the Duala language, originated from a Duala dance called the kossa.

steps.action: ) In the same book, the author explains that acc
steps.text: ) In the same book, the author explains that according to Remy Minko Mba (late journalist): "Kossa is a kind of exclamation, a cry of joy that is usually uttered to give more vigor, [and] more energy to the dance.

steps.action: He did this when casually shouting out "kossa ko
steps.text: He did this when casually shouting out "kossa kossa" to children who would dance in reaction.

steps.action: This was to stimulate partners on the dance floo
steps.text: This was to stimulate partners on the dance floor to redouble or increase in fervency in the dance, especially during the frenzied part of the composition, commonly called "secousse" or "balle à terre".

steps.action: Makossa originated when Nelle Eyoum combined the
steps.text: Makossa originated when Nelle Eyoum combined the experiences of Douala ambasse bey, Bassa assiko, and Ghanaian and Nigerian highlife and shouted exclamations to stimulate dancing by children in the 1950s.

steps.action: Its many forms are due to the fact that at each
steps.text: Its many forms are due to the fact that at each region corresponds a particular form of assiko, that is to say a rhythm, a harmonic and melodic course of action as well as a specific orchestration.

steps.action: It is thus that we distinguish a hard assiko, "m
steps.text: It is thus that we distinguish a hard assiko, "muscular", rapid and lilting like Bassa Assiko and Eton Assiko; a cool assiko like Bulu Assiko; and the "Bottle dance" of the North-West Region.

steps.action: In effect, it is characterized by an accelerated
steps.text: In effect, it is characterized by an accelerated rhythm, syncopated particularly rapid and jerky.

steps.action: A way to "cure" through and via methods of dance
steps.text: A way to "cure" through and via methods of dance and sung lyrics which were to morally "rearm" and psychologically balance the family in the funeral.

steps.action: This includes the partners, ascendants, descenda
steps.text: This includes the partners, ascendants, descendants, collaterals, etc.

steps.action: It is a dance of rejoicing and of the Duala peop
steps.text: It is a dance of rejoicing and of the Duala people.

steps.action: It is a dance used as a test of initiation to se
steps.text: It is a dance used as a test of initiation to sexual life as well as marital life.

steps.action: The departure of colonists passed as well by way
steps.text: The departure of colonists passed as well by way of the decolonization of a cultural space and music dominated by the waltz, jerk, twist, and classical music.

steps.action: Light formations (a guitarist, a percussionist,
steps.text: Light formations (a guitarist, a percussionist, and voices), played traditional tunes.

steps.action: In total, makossa has a traditional rhythmic sub
steps.text: In total, makossa has a traditional rhythmic substrate which served as a ramp of launch for the construction of its identity.

steps.action: It is a fusion of Western sounds and traditional
steps.text: It is a fusion of Western sounds and traditional musics.

steps.action: Two major styles emerged: the dance orchestra wi
steps.text: Two major styles emerged: the dance orchestra with brass instruments as well as other Western instruments; and groups more popular with dominant guitar and some musicians.

steps.action: Makossa is a music genre originating in Douala,
steps.text: Makossa is a music genre originating in Douala, Littoral Region, Cameroon in the late 20th century.

steps.action: It is in common time (4/4) for the vast majority
steps.text: It is in common time (4/4) for the vast majority of cases.

steps.action: Language-wise, it is typically sung in French, D
steps.text: Language-wise, it is typically sung in French, Duala or Pidgin English.

steps.action: Tempo-wise, it is typically in between 130 and 1
steps.text: Tempo-wise, it is typically in between 130 and 170 BPM.

steps.action: It along with this acquired the sebene from Cong
steps.text: It along with this acquired the sebene from Congolese rumba.

steps.action: In the 1980s makossa had a wave of mainstream su
steps.text: In the 1980s makossa had a wave of mainstream success across Africa and to a lesser extent abroad.
